frameworks.name: drift-diffusion
frameworks.one_line: Evidence accumulation drift rate captures reference-dependent decision bias.

frameworks.name: signal-detection theory
frameworks.one_line: Read-out bias at decision stage rather than sensory representation.

frameworks.name: efficient coding
frameworks.one_line: Sensory-encoding distortion account rejected for reference repulsion.

core_question: Do reference-induced perceptual biases in motion perception arise from early sensory encoding or from late decision-stage read-out?
key_assumptions: Sensory encoding and decision read-out can be dissociated empirically
Drift-diffusion drift rate indexes the evidence used at the decision stage
Reference repulsion is a representative case of contextual perceptual bias
manipulations: Presence and identity of an explicit motion-direction reference
Task structure across four psychophysical experiments probing direction judgments
key_findings: Reference repulsion behavior is best explained by a decision-stage bias
Drift rate of evidence accumulation depends on the reference, not on the underlying motion estimate
Early sensory-encoding accounts fit the data worse than the decision-bias account
interpretations: Many reported 'perceptual biases' may reflect read-out biases at a decision stage rather than sensory-coding distortions
